title: The next Strait of Hormuz crisis could be even worse
gist: Despite a new US-Iran interim deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the strait remains effectively closed given mine clearance needs and unresolved tolls, Iran retains latent leverage to close it again, and a simultaneous Houthi move to block the Bab al-Mandab Strait would eliminate existing workarounds and trigger a much larger global shipping and inflation shock.

insights.claim: The US and Iran signed an interim peace deal this week promising to remove the US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days and restore pre-war shipping traffic, but the strait is 'not open, nor is it close to opening' because Iran must still clear mines and obstacles, a slow, costly process likely needing external confirmation and support.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors and shippers should not price in a near-term reopening of Hormuz shipping traffic despite the headline deal; the practical timeline is much longer than the 30-day political framing suggests.
insights.quote: The Strait of Hormuz is therefore not open, nor is it close to opening.

insights.claim: Even after demining, ships will face navigational risk without an internationally recognized traffic separation scheme or other security measures, and undetonated ordnance that fell into the sea during the war must also be cleared.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Highlights specific technical prerequisites (traffic separation scheme, ordnance clearance) that serve as concrete milestones to watch for real normalization, beyond diplomatic announcements.
insights.locator: This demining process will be slow and costly

insights.claim: There is unresolved ambiguity over who administers and monetizes the strait going forward: Trump has said passage will be 'permanently toll-free,' but Iranian officials had previously said fees would be charged for unspecified services, and the deal only commits Iran to work with Oman and other littoral states to define future administration.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A toll/fee dispute is a plausible future friction point that could reopen tensions even after shipping formally resumes.
insights.locator: Although President Trump has said passage

insights.claim: Maritime insurers have so far not significantly reduced Hormuz insurance premiums, indicating the insurance market does not yet trust the deal's durability and will require more evidence of US and Iranian commitment before shipping flows resume at scale.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Insurance premium levels are a real-time, market-based leading indicator for whether the Hormuz deal is actually holding, more reliable than diplomatic statements.
insights.locator: So far, insurance companies have not significantly reduced

themes.theme: Latent Iranian leverage and Houthi risk

insights.claim: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens, Iran will retain the technical ability to close it again, and the mere threat of closure may be enough to deter shipping and cause significant disruption at little cost to Tehran.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Reopening does not eliminate the chokepoint risk premium; Iran retains coercive leverage over global shipping and energy markets indefinitely.
insights.locator: Even if the Strait of Hormuz is reopened

insights.claim: The Houthis in Yemen may seek to close the Bab al-Mandab Strait in a future conflict, with early signs already visible: on 8 June they threatened to block Israeli and Israeli-linked ships, and on 10 June a small vessel reportedly harassed a commercial ship near Bab al-Mandab.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The central risk scenario of the piece: a coordinated or coincidental dual-chokepoint crisis (Hormuz plus Bab al-Mandab) is already showing precursor signals, worth active monitoring.

insights.claim: Between 2024 and 2025 the Houthis attacked over 190 commercial ships in the Red Sea, and even after a May 2025 US-Houthi ceasefire ended the attacks, the Houthis have retained the ability to threaten maritime traffic at any time.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A ceasefire does not remove the Houthis' capability or willingness to disrupt shipping again; the threat is structural, not resolved by prior diplomacy.
insights.locator: Shipping in the Red Sea has faced disruption before

insights.claim: Suez Canal vessel traffic dropped by 90% in 2024 amid Houthi attacks on the Red Sea, showing that even the threat of attacks alone — via elevated insurance premiums and crew safety concerns — is enough to collapse a chokepoint's throughput.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Quantifies how severe chokepoint disruption can get from threats alone, a useful benchmark for stress-testing a renewed Bab al-Mandab crisis scenario.
insights.locator: Amid Houthi attacks, vessel traffic through the Suez Canal

insights.claim: Current workarounds for the effectively closed Strait of Hormuz — such as transporting oil overland to Saudi Arabia's Yanbu port on the Red Sea coast — themselves depend on Red Sea access, so renewed insecurity in Bab al-Mandab would eliminate the very alternatives shippers are relying on now.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This is the mechanism that makes a simultaneous Bab al-Mandab disruption categorically worse than a standalone Hormuz crisis: it removes the fallback route, not just adds a second problem.

insights.claim: The Bab al-Mandab Strait serves as the southern gateway to the Suez Canal, so disruption there has a direct knock-on effect on Suez Canal traffic as well.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A single Houthi disruption campaign can simultaneously degrade two major chokepoints (Bab al-Mandab and Suez), compounding the global shipping impact beyond what either disruption alone would cause.
insights.locator: This also has a knock-on effect on another chokepoint

insights.claim: A dual chokepoint disruption would raise transportation costs via higher insurance premiums and longer Cape of Good Hope routings (more fuel, more opex), while congestion at alternative ports adds further delays; reduced Gulf energy exports and longer routes would likely push up oil and gas prices with broad inflationary effects.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Lays out the transmission mechanism from a chokepoint crisis to global inflation, directly relevant for macro and energy positioning if Hormuz/Bab al-Mandab risk escalates.

insights.claim: The costs of a dual chokepoint crisis would fall disproportionately on smaller, import-dependent economies already facing fiscal stress, reducing their access to food, fuel and essential goods and worsening existing humanitarian crises.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frontier and emerging-market importers are the most exposed tail risk from a renewed Hormuz/Bab al-Mandab crisis, not developed-market consumers.
insights.locator: The consequences would not be distributed evenly

insights.claim: Economic and humanitarian pressure could push countries toward bilateral transit negotiations with Iran and the Houthis rather than relying on collective security; India, Pakistan and Malaysia already sought ad hoc passage deals with Tehran during the war, and private companies also pursued individual safe-transit deals.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A prolonged chokepoint crisis could fragment the collective maritime-security response into a patchwork of bilateral deals, weakening multilateral frameworks and giving Iran/Houthis leverage over individual states and firms.
insights.locator: During the war, countries like India, Pakistan, and Malaysia

insights.claim: Existing protective frameworks in the Red Sea area — European naval missions, the IMO's Maritime Security Transit Corridor, and the Djibouti Code of Conduct — already operate but the author implies they are insufficient, calling for governments and industry to move beyond reactive responses.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals a policy gap: current multilateral maritime-security architecture has not proven capable of preventing recurring chokepoint disruption, implying a call for more proactive (not just reactive) measures.
insights.quote: Governments and industry will need to move beyond reactive responses.
